item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only disagreement is about the Keeter classification. It sounds to me like uncontained Euclid and not uncontainable Keter.

Great article, excellent villain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An article about teeth growing all over the body and the scariest thing about it is… $2.6 million.

These doctors are absurdly rich from their clients, and this money must've been insane at that time. But I still… How could anyone be willing to sell their daughter to a questionable dentist for any amount of money? Just what the hell kind of people are these for this transaction to even be possible?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe, but a good part of the horror of these fairies was that they seemingly grew teeth for no reason. Should this explanation be removed for kept in? But it does make sense that while Foundation wasn't able to figure it out, this doctor managed to find the answer by doing more horrifying experiments.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Sooooooooo… I'm not going to reinvent the wheel here. Kaktus has already gone on to explain that the fayries in this skip are instances of SCP-478 and as such should be crosslinked to that particular article. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

Designation: 104 -"Tooth Fayrie"

To this:

Designation: 104 -"Tooth Fayrie"

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are all instances of 1994-2 capable of infecting people, including the removed ones? Also, I'm wondering what that first guy was trying to do, hanging around the tooth fairies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is it that I'm the only one not afraid of teeth?

…because it looks like you assumed that the prior commenters on this page were the full extent of reactions to the article?

There's probably a ton of people who read the article and are not afraid of teeth, just like you. Just because they didn't feel the need to comment about it doesn't mean they're automatically afraid of teeth.

Edit: Actually, now that it's been mentioned, I'm rather fond of spiders. Generally the non-poisonous ones, since being scared of poisonous spiders (especially any in close proximity) isn't being a wimp, it's possessing basic survival skills.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is it that I'm the only one not afraid of teeth? Is it like arachnophobia with you guys? I mean, sure, it's gross to have someone grow teeth all over their body, but…I don't know. If teeth are this scary, I might as well write something about teeth spiders.

They are spiders, but their webs are made of teeth! OOOoooOOOooo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ha! Good joke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was expecting the nasty effects of the SCP to be the fairies getting revenge on humans.

how the fayries draw enamel from the bones of men, and how they utilize this as a source of nutrition.

So, tooth fairies actually eat teeth. That actually makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The inclusion of the Tooth Fairies wasn't only because of the similarities between this and that. I liked the idea of the doctor bastardizing them for science; tooth fairies are a magical, mythical creature that do magical, mythical things, and he chops them up, pulls out their insides, studies it, and then creates a serum from their lifeblood. The effects are similar because one was derived from the other, both in universe and out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really well-written, but neutral vote because I don't like the inclusion of the "tooth fairy" concept - to me, it seems unnecessary and is kind of contrary to the wonderfully-written clinical mad-science tone of the rest of the article. I'd recommend removing that concept.

(I wouldn't worry about the fact that we already have SCP-478: this article is more than well-written enough to stand on its own. Kind of like how I wrote SCP-831, and a while later Smapti wrote SCP-1942. They're superficially similar, but each of them works on their own merits. Same principle applies here.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Teeth shit always gets to me. I actually had to take breaks while reading this because it was freaking me out. Gah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Tooth Fairy tie-in poses an interesting question. In the initial draft of this, djkaktus was worried that this was too similar to the Tooth Fairy. Apparently between that initial draft, and the final, the writer of the Tooth Fairy gave dj permission to tie it in. So my interesting question is this:

When you are writing a concept that has been done before (pretty well, IMO, btw, that was the first article that I felt I wanted Brain Bleach for), how do you deal with it. I feel that you have a few, no-win options:

1) Don't acknowledge the previous article, and suffer all the 'it's just like the previous article.'
2) Have a nod to the old article, such as 'links between this and SCP-xxxx are being researched,' and suffer the 'you're just trying to milk a previous article.'
3) Fully integrate the previous article into new one, and risk, well, the type of comments this has gotten.

While I constantly quote that 'anything can work IF you pull it off well enough,' I like how this pulled it off. The basic concept doesn't really on the Tooth Fairies, but it adds to the history of the piece, along with tying it in to previously established canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tooth horror ought to be a subgenre of body horror at this point. Something about stuff to do with teeth is pretty innately creepy, and this piece operates with a good understanding of that dynamic. In a lesser article, the pictures would have seemed like cheap shock value, but paired with the narrative they really help lend a good sense of immersion to what you're doing.

This also has some nice moves to it. The idea of being unable to eat because you're full of teeth is certainly horrifying enough, but I found the cause of death being systemic, bodily tooth decay to be both clever and nightmarish (those of us that have had a lot of work done are probably cursed with an ability to envision something like that). So you've got originality of body horror going for you, which is always appreciated.

I do have some issues. Like Faraday Cage, I'm not terribly fond of the tooth fairies angle. It detracts from the musty, institutional Eastern Bloc science feel that I love so well, and I find the concept of this dude cutting up fairies to be kind of silly in comparison to the rest of the article. I also could have done without the clearly delineated motivation behind this. Making this about a rich dentist with rich clients who want real teeth gave this a little too much mundanity for what it was, and personally I would have found the recovered logs of a Russian scientist obsessed with teeth for some mysterious reason far creepier in this case. And you walk the line a bit with the experiments; the description of the teenage girl at the end came awfully close to exploitation by my reckoning.

In the end, the craftsmanship that went into this and the solid core concept won the day. This is something that is pretty genuinely horrifying, given extra creepiness by the convincing scientific tone. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not bad, but it reads more like a tale for the Tooth Fairies than an SCP in its own right. It also feels a bit nastier than follows naturally from the concept. I don't quite buy the teeth being all pseudo-enamel, nor the super-viral spread of them. I can buy an evil doctor, but I don't see him immediately jumping to murder with so little motivation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I got some pliers if you want to do something about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How the hell am I supposed to sleep with all these horrible teeth in my head?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Teeeeeeeth D=

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oops! That should be "became". Good catch!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yay! You posted it! And it is finished now too! I have to say, I really enjoyed it kaktus! Congrats. Bravo and +1.

Oh, before I forget.

This fayrie, who he said called itself "Isabella", assisted the doctor in learning the fayrie language and because his personal assistant.

Wasn't quite sure what it was meant to be or otherwise I would have fixed it myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …besides that, there would have then been people complaining about the high similarities to the Tooth Fairy skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh. Oh god. Kaktus, what is wrong with you? This is the most disturbing thing that I have read in a LONG time. I think I might have nightmares.

Enthusiastic +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good body horror, good Keter, and a great villain in that doctor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rasmin Yelkov has no patience for the unexplained and mysterious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not convinced that the inclusion of the tooth fairies is particularly vital or even necessary.
In fact, IMHO the article would read better without them, leaving the origin of the serum partly unexplained and mysterious.
Also the word 'neck' is misspelled in one instance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congratulations on writing the first skip to scare me in months. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone with a long history with dental work, I'm going to tell you that you successfully reverted about 77% of my overcoming of teeth related heebie jeebies.

+1, you son of a bitch.

item.title: Brush your teeth, kiddos
item.content:encoded:
Ok, so, this is "Rot". I received a lot of really great help and advice about this article, but primarily from Dexanote, who gave me permission to reference the Tooth Fairies. It would not have been possible without him, and for that, I'm extremely appreciative.

I wrote this because I work in dental, and have wanted to write a tooth-skip for a long time. I hope it gives you as many heebie-jeebies as it does me :D

Edit: All of the images contained herein are my own, released under Creative Commons.
